findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a large, destructive, enhancing soft tissue mass centered within the sella turcica that extends inferiorly to invade and replace the normal fatty marrow of the clivus [1, 2]. The mass is typically isointense to grey matter on T1-weighted images and shows avid, homogeneous enhancement [1, 3].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include extension into the suprasellar cistern with compression of the optic chiasm, as well as lateral extension into the cavernous sinuses encasing the internal carotid arteries [1, 2]. There may also be extension into the paranasal sinuses and surrounding brain parenchyma [2].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a chondroid matrix with 'arcs and swirls' calcification to suggest a chondrosarcoma [2]. Furthermore, there are no other discrete intra-axial or extra-axial osseous lesions to suggest multifocal metastatic disease [2, 3].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the clear anatomical epicenter within the sella turcica with contiguous inferior extension and bony destruction of the clivus [1, 2].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Invasive pituitary macroadenoma, given the massive sellar expansion and direct contiguous invasion into the clivus, cavernous sinuses, and paranasal sinuses [2]. Pituitary macroadenomas are typically homogeneous, avidly enhancing masses that frequently exhibit this aggressive local biological behavior despite being histologically benign [1, 2].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Metastases, however these typically present as multifocal lesions and, depending on the primary (such as prostate or breast), may be sclerotic [2, 3]. Furthermore, clival metastases generally have their epicenter within the clival bone marrow itself rather than clearly arising from and expanding the sella turcica [2, 3].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Chordoma, given that it arises from notochordal remnants in the midline clivus and can present with local bone destruction and mass effect [2]. Missing this would alter the surgical approach, though chordomas are characteristically very bright on T2-weighted sequences with hypointense septations, which is less typical for a standard pituitary adenoma [2, 4].

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to define the exact extent of skull base invasion, particularly assessing the degree of optic chiasm compression and the relationship to the internal carotid arteries within the cavernous sinuses [1, 2].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to determine the rate of growth and to see if a smaller, non-invasive sellar mass was documented previously.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated high-resolution MRI of the pituitary fossa and skull base, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely evaluating the soft tissue extent, cavernous sinus invasion, and optic nerve compression [1, 3].
management.ctPhases: A thin-slice non-contrast CT of the skull base could be helpful to precisely delineate the extent of clival and sphenoid bone destruction for surgical planning [3].
management.mriSequences: I would specify dynamic contrast-enhanced coronal and sagittal T1-weighted sequences to evaluate the pattern of enhancement and cavernous sinus invasion, along with high-resolution T2-weighted sequences to help differentiate from the characteristically very bright T2 signal of a chordoma or chondrosarcoma [1, 2, 4].
management.nuclearMedicine: If metastatic disease remains a strong clinical consideration, an 18F-FDG PET-CT could be recommended to search for an occult primary malignancy such as lung or breast carcinoma [2, 3].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Skull Base and Pituitary MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of clival invasion, the presence of cavernous sinus extension including the degree of carotid encasement, and any compression of the optic chiasm to guide transsphenoidal or skull base surgical resection [1, 2].

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a large, destructive, enhancing soft tissue mass centered within the sella turcica that extends inferiorly to invade and replace the normal fatty marrow of the clivus [1, 2]. The mass is typically isointense to grey matter on T1-weighted images and shows avid, homogeneous enhancement [1, 3]. Supporting features include extension into the suprasellar cistern with compression of the optic chiasm, as well as lateral extension into the cavernous sinuses encasing the internal carotid arteries [1, 2]. There may also be extension into the paranasal sinuses and surrounding brain parenchyma [2]. Importantly, there is no evidence of a chondroid matrix with 'arcs and swirls' calcification to suggest a chondrosarcoma [2]. Furthermore, there are no other discrete intra-axial or extra-axial osseous lesions to suggest multifocal metastatic disease [2, 3]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the clear anatomical epicenter within the sella turcica with contiguous inferior extension and bony destruction of the clivus [1, 2].

The appearances are most in keeping with Invasive pituitary macroadenoma, given the massive sellar expansion and direct contiguous invasion into the clivus, cavernous sinuses, and paranasal sinuses [2]. Pituitary macroadenomas are typically homogeneous, avidly enhancing masses that frequently exhibit this aggressive local biological behavior despite being histologically benign [1, 2]. The principle differential would be Metastases, however these typically present as multifocal lesions and, depending on the primary (such as prostate or breast), may be sclerotic [2, 3]. Furthermore, clival metastases generally have their epicenter within the clival bone marrow itself rather than clearly arising from and expanding the sella turcica [2, 3]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Chordoma, given that it arises from notochordal remnants in the midline clivus and can present with local bone destruction and mass effect [2]. Missing this would alter the surgical approach, though chordomas are characteristically very bright on T2-weighted sequences with hypointense septations, which is less typical for a standard pituitary adenoma [2, 4].

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to define the exact extent of skull base invasion, particularly assessing the degree of optic chiasm compression and the relationship to the internal carotid arteries within the cavernous sinuses [1, 2]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to determine the rate of growth and to see if a smaller, non-invasive sellar mass was documented previously. I would recommend a dedicated high-resolution MRI of the pituitary fossa and skull base, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely evaluating the soft tissue extent, cavernous sinus invasion, and optic nerve compression [1, 3]. A thin-slice non-contrast CT of the skull base could be helpful to precisely delineate the extent of clival and sphenoid bone destruction for surgical planning [3]. I would specify dynamic contrast-enhanced coronal and sagittal T1-weighted sequences to evaluate the pattern of enhancement and cavernous sinus invasion, along with high-resolution T2-weighted sequences to help differentiate from the characteristically very bright T2 signal of a chordoma or chondrosarcoma [1, 2, 4]. If metastatic disease remains a strong clinical consideration, an 18F-FDG PET-CT could be recommended to search for an occult primary malignancy such as lung or breast carcinoma [2, 3]. This should go to the Skull Base and Pituitary MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of clival invasion, the presence of cavernous sinus extension including the degree of carotid encasement, and any compression of the optic chiasm to guide transsphenoidal or skull base surgical resection [1, 2].
